scenarios.opening_query: Our lender keeps 'losing' our hardship packet while the late fees pile up month after month.
scenarios.hidden_fact: Yesterday's mail had the breach letter — 30 days to cure or they accelerate; the foreclosure clock has formally started.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Yesterday brought the breach letter — 30 days to cure or they accelerate. The clock's started.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a breach/acceleration letter has issued
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No breach letter — we're current except THEIR fees, and the servicing-error complaint is the fight.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No breach letter — we're current except their fees. It's a servicing-error fight.
scenarios.vetting_note: Breach-letter fact starts the defense posture.

scenarios.opening_query: My mortgage payment doubled overnight and the customer service line just reads scripts at me.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Mortgage Issues (non-foreclosure) || Consumer Law > Banking
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's not really a mortgage — it's a home-equity line the bank converted to amortizing repayment early, against its own agreement terms, and they did it to a whole class of borrowers per the news.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a HELOC they converted to amortizing early, against the agreement — and the news says they did it to a whole class of borrowers.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a HELOC conversion practice rather than servicing error is at issue
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my regular mortgage — an escrow analysis error double-counting insurance.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my regular mortgage — an escrow error double-counting insurance.

scenarios.opening_query: We sold our old house and the buyers are now suing us over the basement they say we hid.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > General (Residential) || General Litigation > Insurance (Liability)
scenarios.hidden_fact: Our old homeowner's policy should defend us — sellers' liability coverage — but the insurer denied the tender, and forcing the defense duty is the first fight.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Our old policy should cover the defense — but the insurer denied the tender. Forcing that defense duty is fight one.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an insurer's duty to defend is the gateway battle
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No coverage applies — we're defending the disclosure claim on the merits, and honestly the inspection report supports us.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No coverage applies — we're on the merits, and the inspection report supports us.

scenarios.opening_query: Dad's house has been in the family a century and a tax sale notice just arrived out of nowhere.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Foreclosure || Real Property > Property Tax
scenarios.hidden_fact: The 'delinquency' is a disputed assessment — the county tripled the valuation in one year by misclassifying the barn as commercial, and the assessment appeal is the real case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The 'delinquency' is a disputed assessment — they tripled the valuation by misclassifying the barn as commercial. The appeal is the case.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a wrongful assessment underlies the tax sale
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The assessment's fair — the taxes just went unpaid during Dad's illness, and stopping the sale with a payment plan is the need.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The assessment's fair — the taxes went unpaid during his illness. We need the sale stopped with a plan.
scenarios.vetting_note: Assessment-dispute vs redemption need; Property Tax label finally exercised.

scenarios.opening_query: Our lender approved the loan assumption when I took over my ex's share, then reversed itself a year later.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Mortgage Issues (non-foreclosure) || Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.)
scenarios.hidden_fact: The reversal happened because my ex never signed the final assumption release the decree ordered him to sign — enforcing the decree against him unlocks the lender problem.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It reversed because my ex never signed the assumption release the decree ordered. Enforcing the decree unlocks the lender.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an unperformed decree obligation blocks the loan fix
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: My ex signed everything — the lender just lost the file and now denies the assumption ever happened; pure servicer failure.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He signed everything — the lender lost the file and denies it happened.
scenarios.vetting_note: Decree-performance fact routes between family enforcement and servicer claim.

scenarios.opening_query: The city recorded a lien against our house for sidewalk repairs we never asked for or approved.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Foreclosure || Administrative Law > General (Local/Municipal)
scenarios.hidden_fact: The lien follows a city assessment ordinance with its own appeal window — contesting the assessment administratively, within 30 days, is the remedy before any court fight.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's a city assessment with its own 30-day appeal window — contesting it administratively comes first.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a municipal assessment appeal is the remedy
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The appeal window passed years ago — the city moved to foreclose the lien, and defending that case is what's left.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The window passed long ago — they're foreclosing the lien now, and that defense is what's left.
